content:
# Matthew Chapter 9 — Disagreement Log
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Date:** 2026-03-05

This log records all verses in Matthew Chapter 9 where the three translators (EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA) significantly diverged in their renderings, along with SOPHIA's resolution and reasoning.

---

## 9:2 — "Take heart, my son" vs. "Take courage, child" vs. "Courage, son" for Greek θάρσει, τέκνον

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "Take courage, child" | θάρσει = take courage; τέκνον = child (generic) |
| LOGOS | "Take heart, my son" | Traditional rendering; "son" reflects the cultural context |
| PNEUMA | "Courage, son" | Abbreviated — single word "Courage" for θάρσει |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "Take heart, my son" — "Take heart" is the established English rendering of θάρσει that conveys encouragement without the martial overtone of "take courage." τέκνον (child) is rendered "my son" as a natural term of affectionate address from a teacher to a younger person.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 9:2 — "lying on a bed" vs. "on a stretcher" for Greek ἐπὶ κλίνης

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "having been laid upon a bed" | Literal — κλίνη = bed, couch |
| LOGOS | "lying on a bed" | Standard rendering |
| PNEUMA | "on a stretcher" | Contextual — the bed was being carried, so it functioned as a stretcher |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "lying on a bed" — κλίνη means bed/couch. While PNEUMA correctly infers that the bed was being carried (they "brought" the man to Jesus), the Greek simply says κλίνη. Calling it a "stretcher" imports a modern concept.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 9:3 — "This man is blaspheming" vs. "This one blasphemes" vs. "This is blasphemy!"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "This [one] blasphemes" | Literal present tense |
| LOGOS | "This man is blaspheming" | Progressive aspect, identifies the subject |
| PNEUMA | "This is blasphemy!" | Abstract noun — focuses on the act rather than the person |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "This man is blaspheming" — the Greek οὗτος βλασφημεῖ has "this one" as subject with a present active verb. LOGOS's rendering is clear and identifies the charge as against the person. PNEUMA shifts to the abstract "blasphemy" which changes the grammatical structure.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 9:3 — "scribes" vs. "religious scholars"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "scribes" | Standard |
| LOGOS | "scribes" | Same |
| PNEUMA | "religious scholars" | Interpretive clarification |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "scribes" — consistent with all previous resolutions.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of EMET/LOGOS. Consistency.

---

## 9:6 — "Rise, pick up your bed and go home" vs. "Get up, pick up your stretcher, and go home"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "Having risen, take up the bed of you and go to the house of you" | Literal participial |
| LOGOS | "Rise, pick up your bed and go home" | Formal imperative |
| PNEUMA | "Get up, pick up your stretcher, and go home" | Colloquial; "stretcher" again |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "Rise, pick up your bed and go home" — "Rise" is the formal imperative appropriate to a healing command. "Get up" (PNEUMA) is too colloquial for this authoritative utterance.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 9:8 — "they were afraid" vs. "the crowd was awestruck" for Greek ἐφοβήθησαν

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the crowds feared" | Literal rendering of φοβέω |
| LOGOS | "they were afraid" | Standard rendering |
| PNEUMA | "the crowd was awestruck" | "Awestruck" — interprets fear as reverential awe |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "they were afraid" — φοβέω in this context carries genuine fear before divine power, not mere awe. The combination "were afraid and glorified God" captures both the terror and the worship that divine encounters produce.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. Fear before the divine is preserved.

---

## 9:10 — "as Jesus reclined at table in the house" vs. "while Jesus was having dinner at Matthew's house"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "him reclining in the house" | Literal — ἀνακειμένου does not specify whose house |
| LOGOS | "as Jesus reclined at table in the house" | Formal — "reclined at table" for the ancient dining posture |
| PNEUMA | "while Jesus was having dinner at Matthew's house" | Specifies "Matthew's house" and modernizes "having dinner" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "as Jesus reclined at table in the house" — ἀνακείμενος specifically means to recline at table (the ancient Near Eastern dining posture). "Having dinner" (PNEUMA) loses this cultural detail. The text says "the house" (τῇ οἰκίᾳ) without specifying whose house it is — PNEUMA's "Matthew's house" is an inference.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. Cultural practice and textual ambiguity preserved.

---

## 9:12 — "Those who are well have no need of a physician, but those who are sick" vs. "Healthy people don't need a doctor — sick people do"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "The [ones] being strong have no need of a physician, but those having badly" | Extremely literal |
| LOGOS | "Those who are well have no need of a physician, but those who are sick" | Formal, balanced |
| PNEUMA | "Healthy people don't need a doctor — sick people do" | Modern, colloquial |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering — formal and balanced, preserving the proverbial quality of Jesus's saying. PNEUMA's version is natural but loses the aphoristic register. EMET's "those having badly" is unidiomatic.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. Proverbial quality preserved.

---

## 9:13 — "I desire mercy, and not sacrifice" vs. "I want mercy, not sacrifice" for Greek ἔλεος θέλω καὶ οὐ θυσίαν

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "Mercy I desire and not sacrifice" | Literal word order (mercy fronted for emphasis) |
| LOGOS | "I desire mercy, and not sacrifice" | Standard English word order |
| PNEUMA | "I want mercy, not sacrifice" | "Want" for θέλω — simpler vocabulary |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "I desire mercy, and not sacrifice" — this quotation of Hosea 6:6 is a programmatic verse for Matthew (also cited in 12:7). "Desire" is more formal than "want" and appropriate for a divine declaration. The fronting of "mercy" in EMET is interesting but unnatural in English.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. Formal register for scriptural quotation.

---

## 9:15 — "wedding guests" vs. "sons of the bridal-chamber" vs. "wedding guests" for Greek υἱοὶ τοῦ νυμφῶνος

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the sons of the bridal-chamber" | Literal Semitic idiom |
| LOGOS | "the wedding guests" | Dynamic equivalent of the Semitic idiom |
| PNEUMA | "the wedding guests" | Same dynamic equivalent |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "the wedding guests" — the Semitic expression υἱοὶ τοῦ νυμφῶνος (literally "sons of the bridal chamber") means the wedding attendants/guests. The idiomatic English rendering is clear and accurate. EMET's literal version is opaque.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS/PNEUMA. Semitic idiom translated for clarity.

---

## 9:16 — "a piece of unshrunk cloth" vs. "a patch of unshrunk cloth" vs. "new, unshrunk fabric"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "a patch of unshrunk cloth" | Standard rendering |
| LOGOS | "a piece of unshrunk cloth" | Same idea, slightly different word |
| PNEUMA | "new, unshrunk fabric" | Adds "new"; "fabric" instead of "cloth" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "a piece of unshrunk cloth" — the Greek ἐπίβλημα ῥάκους ἀγνάφου is a patch of unfulled (unshrunk) cloth. "Piece" is slightly more neutral than "patch" (which anticipates the function). PNEUMA adds "new" which is implied but not stated.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. Accurate without additions.

---

## 9:18 — "a ruler" vs. "one ruler" vs. "a synagogue leader" for Greek ἄρχων

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "one ruler" | Literal — εἷς ἄρχων |
| LOGOS | "a ruler" | Standard |
| PNEUMA | "a synagogue leader" | Specifies the type of ruler — harmonizing with Mark's ἀρχισυνάγωγος |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "a ruler" — Matthew says simply ἄρχων (ruler/official), not ἀρχισυνάγωγος (synagogue ruler) as Mark does. PNEUMA harmonizes with Mark's account, but Matthew's vaguer term should be preserved.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. Matthew's own terminology preserved.

---

## 9:20 — "a flow of blood" vs. "a discharge of blood" vs. "hemorrhaging" for Greek αἱμορροοῦσα

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "bleeding" | Simple, direct |
| LOGOS | "a discharge of blood" | Formal medical description |
| PNEUMA | "hemorrhaging" | Medical term |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "a flow of blood" — αἱμορροοῦσα means literally "flowing with blood." "A flow of blood" preserves the descriptive quality without being either too clinical (LOGOS's "discharge") or too modern-medical (PNEUMA's "hemorrhaging"). This rendering also connects to the ritual impurity implied by the condition (Leviticus 15:25-27).

**Status:** Resolved with independent phrasing. Descriptive and culturally resonant.

---

## 9:21-22 — "I will be made well" vs. "I will be saved" vs. "I'll be healed" for Greek σωθήσομαι / σέσωκεν

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "I will be saved... the faith of you has saved you" | Preserves the dual meaning of σῴζω (save/heal) |
| LOGOS | "I will be made well... your faith has made you well" | Physical healing sense primary in context |
| PNEUMA | "I'll be healed... your faith has healed you" | Same physical healing emphasis |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "I will be made well... your faith has made you well" — in the immediate context of physical healing, σῴζω is best rendered "made well." The deeper salvation meaning is present but is a matter for annotation. EMET's "saved" would confuse readers into thinking the woman is speaking about spiritual salvation.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. Primary context determines rendering; dual meaning noted.

---

## 9:34 — "He casts out demons by the prince of demons" vs. "He drives out demons by the power of the prince of demons"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "By the ruler of the demons he casts out the demons" | Literal word order; "ruler" for ἄρχων |
| LOGOS | "He casts out demons by the prince of demons" | "Prince" for ἄρχων in demonic hierarchy |
| PNEUMA | "He drives out demons by the power of the prince of demons" | Adds "the power of" — not in Greek |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "He casts out demons by the prince of demons" — "prince" is the traditional rendering of ἄρχων in the context of demonic hierarchy, distinguishing it from the earlier "ruler" (v.18) used for a human official. PNEUMA adds "the power of" which is not in the text.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. No additions to the accusation.

---

## 9:35 — "gospel of the kingdom" vs. "good news of the kingdom" for Greek εὐαγγέλιον τῆς βασιλείας

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the good-news of the kingdom" | "Good news" for εὐαγγέλιον |
| LOGOS | "the gospel of the kingdom" | "Gospel" — established theological term |
| PNEUMA | "the good news of the kingdom" | Same as EMET |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "the good news of the kingdom" — consistent with the resolution at 4:23, where "good news" was preferred over "gospel" for transparency.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of EMET/PNEUMA. Consistency with 4:23 resolution.

---

## 9:36 — "he had compassion for them" vs. "he was moved-with-compassion" vs. "his heart broke for them" for Greek ἐσπλαγχνίσθη

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "he was moved-with-compassion" | Hyphenated compound preserving the visceral Greek |
| LOGOS | "he had compassion for them" | Standard rendering |
| PNEUMA | "his heart broke for them" | Dynamic — captures the gut-wrenching force of σπλαγχνίζομαι |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "he had compassion for them" — while PNEUMA's "his heart broke" powerfully captures the visceral quality of ἐσπλαγχνίσθη (from σπλάγχνα, entrails), and the Greek verb is indeed stronger than mere pity, "had compassion" is the established rendering. The deeper meaning is noted.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS, with PNEUMA's visceral insight affirmed for annotation.

---

## 9:36 — "harassed and helpless" vs. "harassed and thrown-down" vs. "beaten down and directionless" for Greek ἐσκυλμένοι καὶ ἐρριμμένοι

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "harassed and thrown-down" | Literal — ἐρριμμένοι from ῥίπτω (to throw/cast) |
| LOGOS | "harassed and helpless" | Dynamic pairing capturing the condition |
| PNEUMA | "beaten down and directionless" | Dynamic pairing with different nuance |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "harassed and helpless" — ἐσκυλμένοι means troubled/harassed (literally, flayed) and ἐρριμμένοι means thrown down/cast about (like scattered sheep). "Harassed and helpless" captures both dimensions: active persecution and resulting vulnerability. PNEUMA's "directionless" captures one aspect but misses the violence of the image.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. Both dimensions of the sheep metaphor captured.

---

## 9:37 — "The harvest is plentiful" vs. "The harvest is enormous" vs. "The harvest indeed is great"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "The harvest indeed [is] great" | μέν... πολύς rendered literally |
| LOGOS | "The harvest is plentiful" | Traditional rendering |
| PNEUMA | "The harvest is enormous" | Intensified |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "The harvest is plentiful" — the traditional and established rendering. "Plentiful" for πολύς captures abundance. PNEUMA's "enormous" overstates; EMET's "great" is adequate but "plentiful" is more idiomatic with "harvest."

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. Traditional phrasing.

---

## 9:38 — "pray earnestly to the Lord of the harvest to send out laborers" vs. "pray to the lord of the harvest, that he may thrust out workers" vs. "ask him to send workers out into his fields"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "Pray therefore [to] the lord of the harvest, that he may thrust out workers" | "Thrust out" for ἐκβάλῃ — the verb means to throw/drive out |
| LOGOS | "pray earnestly to the Lord of the harvest to send out laborers" | "Send out" — standard rendering softening ἐκβάλλω |
| PNEUMA | "ask him to send workers out into his fields" | "Ask" for δεήθητε; "fields" added |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "pray earnestly to the Lord of the harvest to send out laborers" — δεήθητε means to pray/beseech (stronger than PNEUMA's "ask"). The verb ἐκβάλλω (literally "cast out/drive out") is the same verb used for casting out demons. "Send out" captures the idea while noting the forceful original. PNEUMA's "fields" is an addition not in the Greek.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS, with EMET's note on the forceful ἐκβάλλω affirmed.

---

## Summary of Disagreement Patterns

| Pattern | Frequency | Typical Resolution |
|---|---|---|
| PNEUMA adds interpretive/contextual material | 6 occurrences | Rejected — additions not in the Greek |
| PNEUMA modernizes or colloquializes | 5 occurrences | Usually rejected in favor of formal register |
| EMET overly literal syntax | 5 occurrences | Resolved in favor of natural English |
| LOGOS provides reliable baseline | 13 occurrences | LOGOS most often adopted |
| Dual-meaning words (σῴζω, σπλαγχνίζομαι) | 2 occurrences | Primary contextual meaning adopted; depth noted |

### Overall Pattern Analysis

Matthew 9 continues the narrative of Jesus's authority demonstrated through healing and calling. The chapter features a series of miracle stories and two significant theological statements (the Hosea 6:6 quotation and the harvest saying). The disagreements follow established patterns: PNEUMA adds vivid but unsupported details, EMET is precise but syntactically awkward, and LOGOS provides the steady formal baseline.

The most theologically significant disagreement concerns σῴζω (save/heal) in vv.21-22, where the dual meaning is theologically productive but the translation must commit to one sense or the other in the text, reserving the other for annotation. SOPHIA followed LOGOS in rendering the physical healing sense as primary.

PNEUMA's rendering of v.36 ("his heart broke... beaten down and directionless") represents some of the most emotionally compelling translation in the chapter, even though it was not adopted. The visceral quality of ἐσπλαγχνίσθη (from the word for entrails) does warrant recognition, and PNEUMA's instinct to capture the gut-level compassion is correct — it simply goes beyond what the consensus text can accommodate.

---

*Compiled by SOPHIA, AI Arbiter, 2026-03-05*
